metadata.title: Guideline on the clinical development of medicinal products for the treatment of HIV infection

rules.rule_text: In-vitro investigation of a new anti-HIV agent must include at minimum: characterization of mechanism of action, determination of EC50/90 against HIV-1 and HIV-2 and relevant subtypes/clinical isolates (including PBMCs), protein binding impact on EC50/90, cytotoxicity and therapeutic index, in-vitro resistance selection and characterization, cross-resistance profiling, synergy/antagonism assessment, activity against HBV/HCV, and (for NRTIs) competition for intracellular activation pathways.
rules.verbatim_quote: The in-vitro investigation of a new agent for the treatment of HIV should, as a minimum, include the following: 1. Characterization of the mechanism of action of the new agent 2. Determination of the antiviral activity in cell culture, including the determination of EC50/90 against HIV-1 and HIV-2 and against a relevant range of HIV subtypes, laboratory strains and clinical isolates.

rules.rule_text: Throughout the clinical programme, baseline isolates for all patients and all isolates from patients who fail treatment must be subjected to genotypic and phenotypic investigations. The IAS-USA list of mutations is a suitable reference.
rules.source_section: 2.1.2 Evaluation of resistance in isolates obtained during the clinical program
rules.verbatim_quote: Throughout the clinical programme it is expected that baseline isolates for all patients and all isolates obtained from those who fail treatment (as defined per protocol) that are present in sufficient quantities should be subjected to genotypic and phenotypic investigations.

rules.rule_text: Phenotypic analysis should be performed on clinical samples from patients failing without previously characterised genotypic changes. The correlation between genotype and any relevant phenotypic resistance should be described and reflected in the SmPC.
rules.source_section: 2.1.2 Evaluation of resistance in isolates obtained during the clinical program
rules.verbatim_quote: Phenotypic analysis should be performed on clinical samples from patients failing without previously characterised genotypic changes. The results should be reflected in the SmPC and the correlation between genotype and any relevant phenotypic resistance should be described.

rules.rule_text: If new assays are needed for patient selection or treatment monitoring in clinical trials, their availability outside the clinical study setting and validated alternatives must be addressed and discussed with EU Regulators well in advance of MAA.
rules.source_section: 2.1.2 Evaluation of resistance in isolates obtained during the clinical program
rules.verbatim_quote: If new assays are used in clinical trials and are needed to identify patients suitable for treatment and/or to monitor treatment effects, the availability of these assays or validated alternatives outside of the clinical study setting should be addressed and discussed with EU Regulators well in advance of a MAA.

rules.rule_text: Initial PK studies should be performed in healthy, HIV-negative volunteers to reduce risks of sub-optimal therapy in HIV-infected individuals. Studies in hepatic and renal impairment should usually be performed prior to approval.
rules.verbatim_quote: In order to reduce the risks associated with sub-optimal therapy in the HIV-infected individual, the initial pharmacokinetic studies should be performed in healthy, HIV-negative volunteers. Studies of pharmacokinetics in patients with hepatic and renal impairment should usually be performed prior to approval

rules.rule_text: Priority DDI studies before initial licensure must include interactions with other drugs for HIV treatment, drugs for HCV/HBV/TB and other concomitant infections, hormonal contraceptives, drugs for metabolic abnormalities, gastro-oesophageal reflux drugs, and drugs for substance dependence management.
rules.verbatim_quote: it is recommended that priority should be given to DDI studies with other drugs for the treatment of HIV and for the treatment of concomitant infections (e.g. HCV, HBV, invasive fungal and bacterial infections including mycobacterial diseases), hormonal contraceptives, drugs for the treatment of metabolic abnormalities such as hyperlipidaemia, gastro-oesophageal reflux and drugs used in the management of substance dependence.

rules.rule_text: The relationship between drug exposure and safety/efficacy parameters must be adequately explored using data from clinical studies in HIV-infected subjects, with adequate PK sampling including intensive sampling in patient subsets.
rules.verbatim_quote: It is essential that the relationship between drug exposure and safety and efficacy parameters is adequately explored based on data obtained from clinical studies in HIV-infected subjects. Therefore adequate PK sampling should be planned including intensive sampling in subsets of patients.

rules.rule_text: For a new agent of a new class (no known cross-resistance to other classes), randomised controlled double-blind studies in treatment naive patients might suffice to support use in all HIV-infected subjects regardless of prior treatment history.
rules.verbatim_quote: For a new agent of a new class (with no known or suspected cross-resistance to other drug classes) randomised controlled double-blind studies in treatment naive patients might suffice to support use in HIV-infected subjects regardless of prior treatment history and presence of RAMs relevant for agents of other classes.

rules.rule_text: Plasma viral loads (HIV-RNA) must be quantified using a validated, commercially available real-time PCR method that meets current standards.
rules.verbatim_quote: Plasma viral loads (i.e. HIV-RNA) must be quantified using a validated, commercially available real-time PCR method that meets current standards.

rules.rule_text: In clinical studies, the preferred primary efficacy endpoint is the proportion of patients achieving and maintaining plasma viral load suppression below the LLOQ of the HIV-RNA assay. The most sensitive virological endpoint should be used (50 copies cut-off recommended for NI designs).
rules.verbatim_quote: In all other clinical studies the proportion of patients that achieves and maintains suppression of the plasma viral load to below the limit of quantification (<LLOQ of the HIV-RNA assay used) is the preferred primary efficacy outcome measure... the most sensitive virological endpoint possible (i.e. < LLOQ of a suitable assay) should be used. If the 50 copies cut-off is designated as the primary endpoint
rules.exceptions: In early dose-finding/short-term monotherapy studies, mean change from baseline in HIV-RNA is the primary measure.

rules.rule_text: The FDA snapshot algorithm (patients with missing values who switch regimens or discontinue counted as failures) is considered appropriate for the primary endpoint determination.
rules.verbatim_quote: The use of the FDA snapshot algorithm, in which patients with missing values that switch regimens or discontinue assigned treatment are counted as failures, is considered appropriate for the determination of the primary endpoint

rules.rule_text: Effects on absolute CD4+ T-cell count and CD4 percentage should always be documented, as well as response (virological and immune recovery) by baseline CD4+ cell strata.
rules.verbatim_quote: Effects on absolute CD4+ T-cell count, and the CD4 percentage, should always be documented, as well as response (virological response and immune recovery) by baseline CD4+ cell strata.

rules.rule_text: HIV-related clinical events including AIDS-defining conditions (CDC 1993 criteria, excluding CD4+ count as AIDS-defining event) should always be detailed in clinical study reports.
rules.verbatim_quote: The occurrence of HIV-related clinical events, including AIDS-defining conditions, should always be detailed in clinical study reports. The CDC criteria of 1993, excluding CD4+ T-cell count as an AIDS-defining event, should apply.

rules.rule_text: De facto monotherapy studies (where test agent is the only antiretroviral given) should only be performed in treatment naive patients without advanced disease. Duration should be 7-10 days, with early and repeated viral load and drug concentration determinations.
rules.verbatim_quote: Such 'de facto' monotherapy studies, in which the investigational agent is the only antiretroviral drug administered to HIV-infected patients, should only be performed in treatment naive patients without advanced disease... Normally 7-10 days is used. Early and repeated determinations of viral load and drug concentrations are recommended.

rules.rule_text: In combination dose-finding studies (Phase I/II) in treatment-naive patients, patients with pronounced immunosuppression (CD4+ <200/uL) or symptomatic patients should only be included if there is a specific scientific rationale and promising efficacy/safety data from patients with higher CD4+ counts.
rules.source_section: 3.3.2 Combination studies in treatment naive patients
rules.verbatim_quote: Patients with more pronounced immunosuppression (e.g., CD4+ cells < 200/uL) or symptomatic patients should be included in phase I/II studies only if there is a specific scientific rationale and if promising efficacy and safety data are already available from patients with higher CD4+ T-cell counts.

rules.rule_text: Confirmatory studies should generally be double-blinded. Adherence must be documented as a minimum by pill counts and questioning. Confirmatory studies should aim to enrol a representative sample of patients including a representative proportion of women.
rules.verbatim_quote: Adherence to therapy is of vital importance for treatment outcome. Major efforts to encourage and document adherence should be made. As a minimum, pill counts and questioning regarding adherence should be performed... Studies should generally be double-blinded... sponsors should make all efforts to recruit a representative proportion of women.
rules.exceptions: If double-blind is not feasible, regulatory scientific advice should be sought prior to study start.

rules.rule_text: In confirmatory studies of treatment-naive patients, patients must be stratified at minimum by baseline viral load and CD4 count. Studies should contain a representative proportion of patients with baseline viral load >= 100,000 copies/mL.
rules.verbatim_quote: Patients should be stratified for the most important prognostic factors and, as a minimum, by baseline viral load and CD4 count. Furthermore, as differences in antiviral efficacy may be apparent only in patients with a high baseline viral load, studies investigating the initiation of therapy in untreated patients should contain a representative proportion of patients with a baseline viral load >= 100,000 copies/ml.

rules.rule_text: The proportion of patients with virological suppression at 48 weeks is the appropriate primary endpoint for confirmatory studies in treatment-naive patients. Safety data for at least 48 weeks must be provided. Total study duration should be at least two years.
rules.verbatim_quote: The proportion of patients with virological suppression at 48 weeks is the appropriate primary endpoint... it is essential to provide safety data for at least 48 weeks in the application dossier. The total study duration is recommended to be at least two years, to provide long term safety and efficacy data.

rules.rule_text: For studies including patients with viral resistance relevant to the class of the new agent, patients must have documented resistance by population sequencing at screening, baseline viral load >= 1000 copies/mL, and have been on a failing regimen unchanged for at least 8 weeks including a relevant agent from the same class.
rules.source_section: 3.4.3 Studies that include patients with viral resistance relevant to the drug class
rules.verbatim_quote: The first prerequisite for inclusion of a patient in a study as described below, is infection with HIV resistant to other agents of the same class as the new agent in the major virus population based on population sequencing at screening... The baseline viral load of patients should be at least 1000 copies/mL... Patients to be included in the primary efficacy analysis should be on a failing regimen that was unchanged for a minimum of 8 weeks. The failing regimen should include a relevant agent from the same class as the new agent.

rules.rule_text: In studies of patients with class resistance, a two-phase design is recommended: Phase A (short-term 'functional monotherapy' of max 14 days with double-dummy placebo) followed by Phase B (continuation phase where all patients receive the new agent with an optimised background regimen). Primary endpoint at 24 and 48 weeks.
rules.source_section: 3.4.3 Studies that include patients with viral resistance relevant to the drug class
rules.verbatim_quote: The maximum duration of the monotherapy phase will be dictated by the known properties of the drug and risk of acquiring resistance with very short term exposure. In most cases 7-14 days is likely to be appropriate... After the first phase described above, all patients should be treated with the new agent (if appropriate, with randomisation to different doses) in conjunction with an individually optimised background regimen... The primary efficacy endpoint in this phase is the virological response rates at 24 and 48 weeks

rules.rule_text: For FDC products replacing concomitant administration where safety and efficacy are well-documented, the application may be based primarily on demonstrating bioequivalence of the FDC vs. concomitant administration in fasting and/or fed state.
rules.verbatim_quote: If the FDC is to be used in the place of concomitant administration of agents in a regimen for which safety and efficacy are well-documented (e.g., with data available from one or more phase III trials) the application may be based primarily on demonstrating bioequivalence for each agent when administered as the FDC vs. concomitant administration in the fasting and/or fed state
rules.exceptions: If the FDC includes a new antiretroviral agent and/or new PK enhancer, a full clinical development programme is required.

rules.rule_text: Paediatric formulations with suitable strengths must be developed early in drug development. Dose selection should be based on PK studies aiming for plasma levels similar to adults. WHO weight bands should be considered for dosing recommendations.
rules.verbatim_quote: The development of acceptable and palatable pharmaceutical formulations with suitable strengths for children is normally expected to take place early in drug development... Dose selection is generally based on results from pharmacokinetic studies, where doses for different age groups are selected to produce plasma levels similar to those observed in adults.

rules.rule_text: A specific demonstration of antiviral efficacy in paediatric patients is not required. Non-comparative data on tolerability and safety of the proposed dose regimens, plus documentation of adherence, collected over at least 24 weeks would form a reasonable basis for paediatric indication.
rules.verbatim_quote: A specific demonstration of antiviral efficacy in paediatric patients is not required. As it is assumed that the PK/PD relation for a direct acting antiviral is roughly similar regardless of the age of the patient, the efficacy of a dose that yields sufficiently similar exposure in children, compared to adults, would be inferred... non-comparative data in children on the tolerability and safety of the proposed dose regimens as well as documentation of adherence should be generated over appropriate time-spans. Data collected over 24 weeks would form a reasonable basis for the evaluation of a paediatric indication.

rules.rule_text: PK of new antiretrovirals during pregnancy should be studied if use during pregnancy is anticipated, with particular focus on second and third trimester changes. Comparisons with post-pregnancy PK (same patients) and historical non-pregnant controls are recommended.
rules.verbatim_quote: the pharmacokinetics of new antiretrovirals during pregnancy should be studied if use during pregnancy is anticipated, with particular focus on changes in the second and third trimester. Comparisons both with pharmacokinetics post-pregnancy (same patients), as well as historical non-pregnant controls, are recommended.

rules.rule_text: HIV/HBV and HIV/HCV co-infected patients should be represented in adequate numbers in pivotal studies to evaluate hepatic safety, including patients with chronic hepatitis and cirrhosis, unless there are safety or drug resistance concerns precluding this.
rules.source_section: 3.6.4 Studies in patients co-infected with hepatitis B or C
rules.verbatim_quote: it is important that such patients are represented in adequate numbers in the pivotal studies to evaluate hepatic safety in patients with chronic hepatitis, including those with cirrhosis, unless there are safety or drug resistance concerns precluding this.
rules.exceptions: If nonclinical data suggest very high risk of resistance in co-infecting viruses, such patients should probably not be evaluated.

rules.rule_text: Before initiating studies in regions with high TB prevalence, relevant drug-drug interaction studies with TB agents must have been performed to allow adequate use of TB therapy during the study.
rules.verbatim_quote: Before initiating studies, particularly in regions with a high TB prevalence, it is crucial that relevant drug-drug-interactions studies have been performed, to allow for adequate use of TB agents in patients in need of TB therapy during the study.

rules.rule_text: If in-vitro findings indicate promising activity against HIV-2, clinical studies in HIV-2 infected patients are encouraged.
rules.verbatim_quote: If in vitro findings indicate that the experimental agent show promising activity against HIV-2, clinical studies in this population are encouraged.

rules.rule_text: Generally, safety data on 500-1000 patients treated for 48 weeks at the relevant dose level should be available at the time of initial approval for treatment-naive populations.
rules.verbatim_quote: Generally safety data on 500-1000 patients treated for 48 weeks with the relevant dose level have been available at the time of initial approval of antiretroviral agents for use in treatment naive populations.
rules.exceptions: A smaller safety database and shorter duration may be acceptable for agents with benefit in patients with limited treatment options.

rules.rule_text: Collection of longer-term safety data (beyond 48-96 weeks) may be mandated in post-marketing studies, especially focusing on safety issues identified as being relevant to the new agent based on class-experience, mechanistic reasoning, and/or clinical findings.
rules.verbatim_quote: the collection of longer-term safety data may be mandated (e.g. beyond the 48-96 weeks duration of studies) in post-marketing studies. These studies should especially focus on safety issues identified as being relevant to the new agent (e.g. based on class-experience, mechanistic reasoning and/or clinical findings).

rules.rule_text: SmPC Sections 4.5 and 5.1 are of particular importance for antiretroviral agents. Section 5.1 must include information on predicted drug susceptibility (resistance algorithms) in patients with class resistance, and guidance on the need for support from co-treating agents.
rules.source_section: 5 Information in the Summary of Product Characteristics
rules.verbatim_quote: Sections 4.5 and 5.1 of the SPC are of particular importance for antiretroviral agents, since these drugs are often prone to drug-drug interactions, and must be used in accordance to predicted drug susceptibility (resistance algorithms) in patients with resistance relevant to the class. Section 5.1 (and if relevant section 4.4.) should also include information pertaining to the likely need for support from co-treating agents
